docs.title: 🏥 Wits MedWiki
docs.text:
Clinical knowledge for Wits medical students, interns, and junior doctors.

Evidence-based summaries, clinical skills guides, and Wits-specific departmental protocols — structured using the Diataxis documentation framework.

docs.title: Content Framework
docs.text:
Every article belongs to one of four types:

🎓 Tutorial Learning-orientedGuides the learner through a topic step-by-step. Suitable for bedside teaching and self-directed learning. 🔧 How-To Guide Task-orientedHelps a practitioner accomplish a specific procedure or clinical task. Assumes competence. 📖 Reference Information-orientedFactual, authoritative, structured for quick lookup. Includes Wits protocols and drug charts. 💡 Explanation Understanding-orientedExplains concepts, mechanisms, and context. Deepens clinical reasoning.

docs.title: Departments
docs.text: 🫀 Internal Medicine DKA, CCF, AKI, ABG, fluid management 🔪 Surgery Theatre prep, wound care, perioperative 👶 Paediatrics Neonatal, fever, growth, resuscitation 🚨 Emergency & Trauma RSI, ATLS, resuscitation, toxicology 🤰 Obs & Gynae Antenatal, labour, postnatal, gynaecology 🧠 Psychiatry Assessment, pharmacology, Mental Health Act 🦴 Orthopaedics Fractures, compartment syndrome, casting 💧 Urology Catheters, retention, haematuria 👂 ENT Epistaxis, tonsillitis, airway emergencies 👁️ Ophthalmology Red eye, visual loss, ocular emergencies

docs.title: For Contributors
docs.text:
See the Contributing Guide for how to write and submit articles, how to use the Diataxis framework, and how to get your article reviewed and approved.

Educational Disclaimer

Content on Wits MedWiki is for educational purposes only. Always verify against current institutional guidelines and senior clinical advice before applying information to patient care. Wits-specific protocols should be confirmed with your department.

docs.title: Contributing to Wits MedWiki
docs.text: Thank you for contributing to Wits MedWiki. This guide explains how to write articles correctly, what format to use, and how the review process works.

docs.title: The Review Workflow
docs.text:
You write article (Markdown file)
        ↓
Submit via GitHub Pull Request
        ↓
Assigned reviewer (senior/consultant)
        ↓
Approved → merged → live on site

All articles must be reviewed and approved by a senior clinician or consultant before they appear on the site.

docs.title: Diataxis — Choose the Right Article Type
docs.text:
Every article must be one of four types. Choosing the wrong type is the most common mistake. Ask yourself: what does the reader need right now?

Type Reader's need Filename prefix Example 🎓 Tutorial To learn tutorial- Tutorial: Approach to the breathless patient 🔧 How-To To do a specific task howto- How to insert a chest drain 📖 Reference To look something up ref- Wits DKA Protocol 💡 Explanation To understand explanation- Understanding heart failure pathophysiology

Diataxis quick test

  • "A medical student needs to learn this for the first time" → Tutorial
  • "An intern needs to perform this procedure right now" → How-To
  • "A doctor needs the Wits dose/protocol quickly" → Reference
  • "A student wants to understand why something happens" → Explanation

docs.title: File Naming Convention
docs.text:
docs/{department}/{type}-{short-descriptive-name}.md

Examples: 

docs/internal-medicine/tutorial-breathless-patient.md
docs/internal-medicine/ref-dka-protocol.md
docs/surgery/howto-surgical-scrub.md
docs/paediatrics/explanation-febrile-child.md

Rules: - Lowercase only - Hyphens instead of spaces - Always start with the type prefix (tutorial-, howto-, ref-, explanation-)

docs.title: Article Frontmatter (Required)
docs.text:
Every article must start with this YAML block:

---
title: "Full Article Title"
description: "One sentence summary shown in search results"
author: Dr A. Mokoena
editor: Dr S. Pillay
date: 2025-11-14
version: 2
status: approved
tags:
  - Respiratory
  - Cardiovascular
  - Evidence-Based
---
Field Required Notes title ✅ Full title as it appears on the page description ✅ One sentence, shown in search author ✅ Original content creator editor ✅ Reviewer/approver for this version date ✅ Date of this version (YYYY-MM-DD) version ✅ Increment for each approved change status ✅ draft, review, or approved tags Recommended See tag list in mkdocs.yml

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: Copy the appropriate template below for your article type.

docs.title: 🎓 Tutorial Template
docs.text:
---
title: "Tutorial: [Topic Name]"
description: ""
author: 
editor: 
date: YYYY-MM-DD
version: 1
status: draft
tags: []
---

## Learning Objectives

- Objective 1
- Objective 2
- Objective 3

## Prerequisites

What the reader should already know before starting this tutorial.

## Background

Brief contextual introduction.

## Step 1 — [First Step]

Detailed explanation.

## Step 2 — [Second Step]

Detailed explanation.

## Step 3 — [Third Step]

Detailed explanation.

## Summary

Key takeaways.

## Test Yourself

- Question 1?
- Question 2?

## References

1. Author et al. *Title*. Journal/Book, Year. [Link](https://url)

## Overview

What this guide helps you accomplish.

## Indications

When to perform this.

## Contraindications

When NOT to perform this.

## Equipment

- Item 1
- Item 2

## Steps

1. First step
2. Second step
3. Third step

## Aftercare

What to do after.

## Complications

| Complication | Management |
|---|---|
| Complication 1 | Action 1 |

## Tips & Pitfalls

- Tip 1
- Common mistake to avoid

## References

1. Reference 1

docs.text:

description: ""
author:

    This protocol reflects current practice at Wits-affiliated hospitals.
    Confirm with your department before use.

## Overview / Definition

## Classification

|---|---|

## Key Values / Thresholds

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|

Step-by-step institutional guidance.

## Monitoring

author:

version: 1
status: draft
tags: []
---

## What Is It?

Plain-language explanation.

## Why Does It Matter Clinically?

## Pathophysiology / Mechanism

## Analogy or Mental Model

## Common Misconceptions

- **Misconception** — Correction

## Connections to Other Concepts

## Key Points

- Point 1
- Point 2

## References

1. Reference 1

docs.title: Submitting Your Article
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository on GitHub
  2. Create a new branch: git checkout -b add/internal-med-dka-tutorial
  3. Add your .md file in the correct folder
  4. Add your article to the nav: section in mkdocs.yml
  5. Open a Pull Request with a short description
  6. A reviewer will be assigned automatically
  7. Address any feedback, then it gets merged

docs.title: Style Guide
docs.text:
• Write in plain, clear English — your reader may be stressed and time-pressured
  • Use bold for key terms on first use
  • Use tables for comparisons and drug doses
  • Use admonitions for warnings: !!! warning "Title"
  • Keep sentences short
  • Avoid jargon unless defined
  • Use South African drug names and Wits-specific doses where they differ from international guidelines

Clinical accuracy

You are responsible for the accuracy of what you submit. Inaccurate clinical information can cause patient harm. If unsure, mark as status: draft and flag for expert review.

Always use ABCDE

In any emergency, return to ABCDE when uncertain. Treat life threats as you find them — don't wait to complete the assessment before acting.

docs.title: How to Perform RSI (Rapid Sequence Intubation)
docs.text: ✍️ Author: Dr K. van der Berg  |  ✏️ Editor: Dr A. Mokoena  |  📅 2025-03-01  |  📌 Version 3  |  APPROVED

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
• Failure to oxygenate or ventilate
  • Airway protection — GCS ≤8, aspiration risk
  • Anticipated deterioration (burns, angioedema, major trauma)
  • Requirement for controlled ventilation (raised ICP, status epilepticus)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
No absolute contraindications in a dying patient

In a patient in extremis, the benefits of securing the airway outweigh all risks.

Relative contraindications requiring modified approach:

  • Predicted difficult airway — consider awake intubation
  • Suxamethonium contraindications (see below) — use rocuronium

docs.title: Equipment (STOP MAID)
docs.text: Letter Equipment S Suction — Yankauer, switched on T Tube — correct size ETT (men: 8.0, women: 7.5), one size smaller O Oxygen — BVM + reservoir, 15L/min P Positioning — ramped, sniffing position M Monitoring — SpO₂, ETCO₂, ECG, BP A Airway adjuncts — laryngoscope (direct + video), bougie, LMA I IV access — large bore, confirmed patent D Drugs — drawn up, labelled (see below)

docs.title: 1. Preparation
docs.text: Assign roles before starting: intubator, drug nurse, cricoid, BVM. Team brief: "Plan A is direct laryngoscopy. If I can't intubate, Plan B is video laryngoscopy. If I can't ventilate, we go to surgical airway."

docs.title: 2. Preoxygenation
docs.text:
• 100% O₂ via non-rebreather mask, minimum 3 minutes
  • Target SpO₂ >94% before induction
  • Sit patient upright (45°) if possible — increases FRC

docs.title: 3. Pretreatment (optional, case-dependent)
docs.text:
• Fentanyl 3 µg/kg IV — attenuates sympathetic response to laryngoscopy; use in suspected raised ICP
  • Atropine 0.02 mg/kg IV — in children <1 year to prevent bradycardia from suxamethonium

docs.title: 4. Paralysis + Induction (simultaneous)
docs.text:
Give induction agent immediately followed by neuromuscular blocker.

Wits ED Standard Doses:

Drug Dose Notes Ketamine (induction) 1.5–2 mg/kg IV Preferred; maintains BP; bronchodilator Propofol (induction) 1.5–2 mg/kg IV Avoid if hypotensive Suxamethonium (paralytic) 1.5 mg/kg IV Onset 45–60s; duration 8–10 min Rocuronium (if Sx CI) 1.2 mg/kg IV Onset 60–90s; reversed by sugammadex

Suxamethonium contraindications: Hyperkalaemia, crush injury >48h, burns >48h, denervation injury, known myopathy, malignant hyperthermia history, pseudocholinesterase deficiency.

docs.title: 5. Protection (Cricoid Pressure)
docs.text: Apply Sellick's manoeuvre once patient loses consciousness. Release immediately if it impedes laryngoscopy.

docs.title: 6. Placement
docs.text:
• Laryngoscopy after fasciculations cease (~45–60s after suxamethonium)
  • Pass ETT under direct vision
  • Inflate cuff to 20–25 cmH₂O
  • Confirm placement: 5-point auscultation + waveform capnography (gold standard)
  • Secure tube, note depth at teeth

docs.title: 7. Post-Intubation Care
docs.text: Action Detail Ventilator settings TV 6–8 mL/kg IBW; RR 12–16; FiO₂ 1.0 initially Post-intubation sedation Morphine 0.1 mg/kg + midazolam 0.05 mg/kg CXR Confirm tube position (5cm above carina) NG tube Decompress stomach Head of bed 30° elevation

docs.title: Failed Airway Algorithm
docs.text:
Can't intubate → attempt ×2 maximum
        ↓
Can ventilate with BVM?
   YES → video laryngoscopy or supraglottic airway (LMA)
   NO  → CICO (Can't Intubate Can't Oxygenate)
              ↓
        Surgical airway (scalpel-finger-bougie cricothyroidotomy)

CICO is a surgical emergency

If SpO₂ is falling and you cannot ventilate, do not attempt intubation a third time. Call for surgical airway immediately.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Roberts & Hedges. Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, 7e. Elsevier. Link
  2. Wits Emergency Department RSI Protocol (2024). Internal document.
  3. Weingart SD, Levitan RM. Preoxygenation and prevention of desaturation. Ann Emerg Med 2012;59(3):165–175. PubMed

docs.title: Knowledge
docs.text:
• [ ] All medical student competencies above
  • [ ] Ludwig's angina — diagnosis and emergency management
  • [ ] Epiglottitis — adult and paediatric — diagnosis and airway management
  • [ ] Deep neck space infections — anatomy, danger spaces, management
  • [ ] Sudden sensorineural hearing loss — emergency treatment
  • [ ] Nasal fracture — assessment and management

docs.title: Clinical Skills
docs.text:
• [ ] Anterior nasal packing for epistaxis
  • [ ] Aural toilet and wicking
  • [ ] Removal of simple ENT foreign bodies (supervised)

Airway compromise

Ludwig's angina, epiglottitis, and deep neck infections can rapidly obstruct the airway. Call senior ENT and anaesthetics early — do not wait for complete obstruction.

docs.title: How to Use This Page
docs.text:
Self-assessment

Work through each section and tick off competencies as you achieve them. For each unticked item, find the relevant article on this wiki or ask your registrar/consultant for a teaching session.

Adding content

If a competency doesn't have a linked article yet, consider writing one. See the Contributing Guide.

docs.title: Wits Protocol: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
docs.text:
✍️ Author: Dr S. Pillay  |  ✏️ Editor: Dr N. Dlamini  |  📅 2024-08-20  |  📌 Version 4  |  APPROVED

Wits-Specific Protocol

This protocol reflects current practice at Wits-affiliated hospitals (Charlotte Maxeke, Helen Joseph, Chris Hani Baragwanath). Confirm with your department before use.

docs.title: Diagnostic Criteria
docs.text:
All three must be present:

  • Blood glucose >11 mmol/L (or known diabetes mellitus)
  • Ketonaemia ≥3 mmol/L or urine ketones ≥2+
  • pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <15 mmol/L

Euglycaemic DKA

DKA can occur with near-normal glucose in patients on SGLT-2 inhibitors (e.g. empagliflozin, dapagliflozin). Maintain high clinical suspicion.

docs.title: Severity Classification
docs.text: Severity pH Bicarbonate (mmol/L) GCS Mild 7.25–7.30 15–18 15 Moderate 7.00–7.24 10–14 <15 Severe <7.00 <10 <12

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
First 4 hours4–24 hoursResolution criteria
  • Hourly capillary blood glucose
  • Hourly urine output (catheterise if anuric or obtunded)
  • Hourly capillary ketones (if available)
  • 2-hourly electrolytes (especially K⁺)
  • Continuous ECG if K⁺ abnormal
  • 2-hourly blood glucose
  • 4-hourly electrolytes
  • 4-hourly ABG/VBG

All three must be met before stopping fixed-rate insulin:

  • Blood glucose <14 mmol/L
  • Ketones <0.3 mmol/L or bicarbonate >18 mmol/L
  • pH >7.3

docs.title: Fluid Resuscitation
docs.text:
Use 0.9% sodium chloride (normal saline) as follows:

Time Volume Rate 0–1h 1 L Stat 1–3h 1 L Over 2h 3–5h 1 L Over 2h 5–9h 1 L Over 4h 9–13h 1 L Over 4h

Switch to 0.45% NaCl + 5% dextrose when blood glucose falls to <14 mmol/L.

Fluid caution

Reduce rate in elderly patients, those with CCF or renal impairment. Target urine output 0.5 mL/kg/hr.

docs.title: Fixed-Rate Insulin Infusion
docs.text:
• 0.1 units/kg/hr of short-acting insulin (Actrapid/regular insulin) in normal saline
  • Do NOT give a bolus dose
  • Do NOT start insulin until K⁺ ≥3.5 mmol/L

Hypokalaemia risk

Insulin drives K⁺ into cells. Starting insulin with K⁺ <3.5 mmol/L can cause life-threatening hypokalaemia and fatal arrhythmia.

docs.title: Potassium Replacement
docs.text: Serum K⁺ (mmol/L) KCl to add per litre of fluid >5.5 None 3.5–5.5 40 mmol <3.5 Stop insulin — replace K⁺ urgently, recheck before restarting

docs.title: Transition to Subcutaneous Insulin
docs.text:
When resolution criteria are met and the patient can eat and drink:

  1. Give SC short-acting insulin 30 min before stopping infusion
  2. If on insulin pre-admission, restart usual regimen
  3. If new diagnosis, refer to diabetes team for initiation

docs.title: Precipitating Causes (FIND IT)
docs.text: Letter Cause F Forgot insulin / non-compliance I Infection (most common — UTI, pneumonia) N New diagnosis of DM D Drugs (steroids, SGLT-2 inhibitors, cocaine) I Ischaemia (MI, stroke) T Trauma / surgery

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Wits Internal Medicine DKA Guideline v4 (2024). Internal document.
  2. JBDS-IP. Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis in Adults (2023). Download
  3. Kitabchi AE et al. Hyperglycemic crises in adult patients with diabetes. Diabetes Care 2009;32(7):1335–1343. PubMed

docs.title: Approach to the Breathless Patient
docs.text: ✍️ Author: Dr A. Mokoena  |  ✏️ Editor: Dr S. Pillay  |  📅 2025-11-14  |  📌 Version 2  |  APPROVED

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
• Identify the common causes of acute dyspnoea
  • Apply a systematic ABCDE approach to the breathless patient
  • Differentiate cardiac vs respiratory vs other causes
  • Know which investigations to order and why

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Basic thoracic anatomy
  • Cardiovascular and respiratory physiology (heart sounds, breath sounds)
  • Ability to measure vital signs

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
Dyspnoea is one of the most common presenting complaints in internal medicine. A structured approach prevents anchoring bias — the tendency to fixate on the first diagnosis that comes to mind and miss other causes.

Don't anchor

Dyspnoea can have multiple simultaneous causes. A patient with COPD can also have a PE. Always complete your systematic assessment.

docs.title: Step 1 — Immediate Assessment (A-B-C-D-E)
docs.text:
Before taking a history, assess for life-threatening compromise:

Parameter Action if abnormal Airway Open, suction, consider adjunct Breathing — RR, SpO₂, work of breathing Apply O₂ if SpO₂ <94%; call for help Circulation — HR, BP, perfusion IV access, fluids if shocked Disability — GCS Protect airway if GCS ≤8 Exposure — look for clues Oedema, rash, cyanosis

docs.title: Onset and Character
docs.text: Feature Suggests Sudden onset at rest PE, pneumothorax, flash pulmonary oedema Gradual over hours–days Pneumonia, decompensated CCF, asthma Gradual over weeks–months Pleural effusion, malignancy, anaemia Pleuritic (worse on inspiration) PE, pleuritis, pneumothorax, pneumonia Orthopnoea/PND Left heart failure Exercise intolerance only Anaemia, deconditioning, early CCF

docs.title: Associated Symptoms
docs.text:
• Fever, cough, purulent sputum → pneumonia
  • Haemoptysis → TB, malignancy, PE
  • Wheeze → asthma, COPD, CCF (cardiac asthma)
  • Leg swelling, calf pain → DVT/PE
  • Chest pain → ACS, PE, pneumothorax, pericarditis

docs.title: Step 3 — Examination
docs.text:
Examine in a systematic order:

  1. General — distress, posture, cyanosis, anaemia, clubbing
  2. Hands — asterixis (CO₂ retention), nicotine staining
  3. Face — JVP (raise head of bed to 45°)
  4. Trachea — midline? Deviated away = tension pneumothorax or large effusion
  5. Chest — inspect, percuss, auscultate
  6. Legs — pitting oedema, calf tenderness

docs.title: Key Percussion and Auscultation Findings
docs.text: Finding Diagnosis Stony dull + reduced breath sounds Pleural effusion Hyper-resonant + reduced breath sounds Pneumothorax Dull + bronchial breathing Consolidation (pneumonia) Fine bibasal crackles + wheeze Pulmonary oedema Widespread wheeze Asthma / COPD

docs.title: Step 4 — Investigations
docs.text:
Order in order of urgency:

ImmediateUrgent bloodsConditional
  • SpO₂ and ABG (if SpO₂ <94% or severe distress)
  • 12-lead ECG (PE pattern, ACS, arrhythmia)
  • CXR (consolidation, effusion, pneumothorax, cardiomegaly)
  • FBC — anaemia, infection
  • U&E — renal function, electrolytes
  • BNP or proBNP — raised in CCF
  • D-dimer — if PE suspected and low-intermediate probability (Wells score)
  • Troponin — if ACS suspected
  • CT pulmonary angiogram — definitive PE diagnosis
  • Echocardiogram — cardiac function, effusion
  • Sputum culture — pneumonia workup
  • Spirometry — not in acute setting

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Step Key action 1 ABCDE — treat threats immediately, apply O₂ 2 History — onset, character, associated symptoms 3 Examination — JVP, trachea, percussion, auscultation 4 Investigations — ECG, CXR, ABG, FBC, BNP, D-dimer

docs.title: Test Yourself
docs.text:
1. A 65-year-old presents with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, and HR 112. Wells score = 5. What is your next step?
  2. A patient with known CCF presents with worsening dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and bibasal crackles. What one investigation most helps confirm decompensation?
  3. A 22-year-old presents with dyspnoea and wheeze. SpO₂ 91% on air. What is your immediate management?

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Longo DL et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21e. McGraw-Hill. Access
  2. NICE NG115: Chronic heart failure in adults (2018, updated 2023). Read guideline
  3. Wells PS et al. Derivation of a simple clinical model to categorize patients with PE. Thromb Haemost 2000;83(3):416–420.

Obstetric emergencies

Shoulder dystocia, PPH, eclampsia, and cord prolapse are time-critical. Know the protocols before you need them.

docs.text:

Ocular emergencies requiring same-day ophthalmology review

  • Chemical injury (irrigate first, refer immediately)
  • Central retinal artery occlusion (within 4 hours)
  • Acute angle-closure glaucoma
  • Penetrating eye injury
  • Sudden painless visual loss

  • [ ] Joint aspiration — knee (supervised)

Compartment syndrome

A missed compartment syndrome causes permanent disability. Pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch, and tense compartments require urgent surgical consultation — do not delay for investigation.

Drug doses

Always calculate paediatric drug doses by weight. Use the Paediatric Drug Dosing Quick Reference and double-check with a senior before administering.

Mental Health Care Act

The South African Mental Health Care Act 17 of 2002 governs involuntary psychiatric treatment. Every intern must be familiar with the process for 72-hour assessment and longer-term involuntary care.

Testicular torsion

Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. Do not wait for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high — time to surgery determines testicular salvage. Call urology immediately.
